facts.text: Wars have shaped borders, governments, technologies, and cultures throughout human history.
The Battle of Marathon in 490 BC became one of the best-known clashes of the ancient world.
The Battle of Thermopylae in 480 BC made the stand of the Spartans legendary.
The Hundred Years' War between England and France actually lasted 116 years.
The Battle of Hastings in 1066 changed English history when William the Conqueror defeated Harold II.
The Napoleonic Wars reshaped Europe in the early 19th century and ended with Napoleon's defeat at Waterloo in 1815.
The American Civil War included major battles such as Gettysburg, Antietam, and Shiloh.
Gettysburg, fought in July 1863, is often considered the turning point of the Civil War.
World War I introduced trench warfare, poison gas, and industrial-scale destruction on an unprecedented level.
The Armistice ending World War I took effect on November 11, 1918, at the 11th hour.
World War II became the deadliest conflict in human history.
D-Day, the Allied invasion of Normandy, took place on June 6, 1944.
The Battle of Stalingrad is widely seen as one of the decisive turning points of World War II.
The Korean War ended in an armistice rather than a peace treaty, meaning the conflict never formally ended.
The Vietnam War deeply changed politics and culture in both Vietnam and the United States.
Wars often accelerate technologies like radar, aviation, medicine, cryptography, and communications.
Military history is not only about generals and victories — it is also about civilians, logistics, supply lines, and the human cost of conflict.
The Battle of Marathon in 490 BC became one of the most famous clashes of the ancient world.
It is often remembered as a landmark event in the Greco-Persian Wars.
Battle of Marathon is one of the most important topics associated with Wars & Battles.

The legacy of Battle of Marathon continues to shape how Wars & Battles is discussed today.
